s52307341.txt.impression:
IMPRESSION:

In comparison with the earlier study of this date, there is an placement of an
endotracheal tube with its tip at the clavicular level, approximately 5.5 cm
above the carina.  The right IJ catheter is been pulled back with the tip now
in the mid to lower SVC.  Increased opacification at the right base most
likely reflects atelectatic changes.  Increasing atelectasis is also seen at
the left base with mild blunting of both costophrenic angles.
Nasogastric tube extends to the distal stomach.  Incidental note is made of a
cervical fusion device.

s57083442.txt.impression:
IMPRESSION:


The right PICC continues to course up into the neck.
The lung volumes has slightly decreased and a moderate to large right pleural
effusion is relatively stable prior radiograph performed 5 hours prior.  Right
basal consolidation is unchanged.

Compared to chest radiographs, ___ through ___ at 09:43.

s53999189.txt.findings:
FINDINGS:  PA and lateral views of the chest were obtained.  The heart is
mildly enlarged with a left ventricular configuration, unchanged from prior.
Lung volumes are low which limits the evaluation.  There appears to ___ ___
B lines which would suggest mild pulmonary interstitial edema.  There is no
frank consolidation, effusion, or pneumothorax.  Mediastinal contour is
notable for atherosclerotic calcification.  Bony structures are intact.
s53999189.txt.impression: IMPRESSION:  Mild interstitial edema, cardiomegaly with LV configuration.

s54701628.txt.impression:
IMPRESSION:

3 images of the torso were submitted.  The first is centered below the
diaphragm and excludes the apex of the chest. It shows an esophageal tube
ending in the low esophagus just above the level of the diaphragm. The second
is essentially unchanged. The third, shows the entire chest, and the
nasogastric tube withdrawn to the junction of the middle and lower third of
the esophagus. Since earlier in the day, mild interstitial edema has cleared.
Moderate cardiomegaly is chronic. Lung volumes remain very low, accounting for
moderately severe atelectasis at the base the right lung. Thoracic aorta is
generally large, but not recently changed. Upper lungs are clear aside from
mild vascular congestion. No pneumothorax. Recent abdominal CT shows small
hiatus hernia, which may account for difficulty in passing the nasogastric
tube into the stomach.

s57980419.txt.findings:
FINDINGS:  Frontal and lateral views of the chest were obtained.  Slight left
base retrocardiac opacity most likely relates to atelectasis, less likely
early infectious process.  Right lung is clear.  There are low lung volumes.
No pleural effusion or pneumothorax is seen.  The cardiac silhouette is not
enlarged.  The aorta is calcified and tortuous.

Degenerative changes are seen along the spine, although not well evaluated.
s57980419.txt.impression:
IMPRESSION:  Mild left base opacity most likely represents atelectasis,
although an early consolidation is not entirely excluded, but felt less
likely.

s58804835.txt.findings:
FINDINGS:  PA and lateral views of the chest are compared to previous exam
from ___.  The lungs are clear of consolidation or large effusion.
There is elevation of the left hemidiaphragm as on prior with less clear
delineation of posterior costophrenic angle, potentially related to a small
effusion versus atelectasis/scarring.  Cardiomediastinal silhouette is within
normal limits.  Osseous structures again notable for post-thoracotomy changes
on the left.  Multiple surgical clips are seen in the left upper abdomen.
s58804835.txt.impression:
IMPRESSION:  No evidence of confluent consolidation.  New mild blunting of the
left posterior costophrenic angle, potentially due to scarring/atelectasis
versus trace effusion.

s51867982.txt.impression:
IMPRESSION:

Lungs are fully expanded and clear.  Cardiac and hilar silhouettes and pleural
surfaces are normal. Fullness in the left cervicothoracic region displacing
the trachea to the right is little changed since at least ___.
Patient has had thyroid ultrasound showing bilateral thyroid lesions. This
could explain the appearance.  Thyroid pathology could also explain chronic
cough. If there is concern for other cervicothoracic pathology, CT scanning
would be necessary.

RECOMMENDATION(S):  If there is concern for other cervicothoracic pathology,
CT scanning would be necessary

s59510034.txt.findings:

s52589579.txt.findings:
FINDINGS:  In comparison with the earlier study of this date, there is again
hyperexpansion of the lungs consistent with COPD, with bullous and fibrotic
changes that make it difficult to evaluate for possible pneumothorax.  A small
right apical pneumothorax cannot be definitely excluded.  Continued relative
increased opacification at the left base with a differential diagnosis as
described in the previous study of atelectasis, fibrosis, aspiration, or
supervening pneumonia depending on the clinical context.

No change in the nodular opacifications in the right upper zone.

s52810882.txt.findings:
FINDINGS:  As compared to the previous radiograph, the frontal image shows no
change.  Massive tortuosity of the thoracic aorta and moderate cardiomegaly
without overt pulmonary edema.  The lateral radiograph, however, shows small
bilateral dorsal pleural effusions, seen in this projection only, as well as
fluid marking of the fissures, suggestive of mild chronic interstitial
pulmonary edema.  No pneumonia.  Minimal scarring at the bases of the right
upper lobe.

s51149204.txt.findings:

fissure.  There is no definite pleural effusion.  No pneumothorax is evident.
The left lung is grossly clear.  The cardiac and mediastinal contours are
normal.  The upper abdominal drains are noted but incompletely evaluated.
s51149204.txt.impression:
IMPRESSION: Patchy opacification throughout the right lung, worse in the right
upper lobe, compatible with multifocal pneumonia.

s54191806.txt.findings:
FINDINGS:

Right subclavian central venous catheter tip terminates in the mid SVC. No
pneumothorax is identified. Cardiac silhouette is within normal limits.
Widening of the superior mediastinum, particularly the right paratracheal
stripe, corresponds to lymphadenopathy and ill-defined soft tissue density
within the mediastinum, better assessed on recent CT. Mild to moderate
pulmonary edema is new compared to the prior radiograph with a layering
moderate size right pleural effusion and small left pleural effusion, also new
from prior. Worsening opacification of the lung bases, particularly on the
left with air bronchograms, reflect regions of compressive atelectasis as seen
on the prior CT.
s54191806.txt.impression:
IMPRESSION:


1. Right subclavian central venous catheter tip in the mid SVC.  No
pneumothorax.
2. Mild to moderate pulmonary edema, new compared to the prior radiograph,
with moderate layering right pleural effusion and small left pleural effusion.
3. Worsening opacification of the lung bases, more so on the left, compatible
with areas of compressive atelectasis.
4. Abnormal widening of the superior mediastinal contour, correlating to
lymphadenopathy and abnormal soft tissue density within the mediastinum as
seen on the prior CT.

s50442068.txt.findings:
FINDINGS:  PA and lateral views of the chest were obtained.  Lungs are
hyperinflated with flattened diaphragms and coarsened reticular markings
compatible with emphysema/COPD.  There is no focal consolidation, pleural
effusion or pneumothorax.  There is prominence of the pulmonary hila, right
greater than left, likely reflective of pulmonary hypertension. Heart size is
top normal.  Bony structures appear intact with dextroscoliosis of the
T-spine.
s50442068.txt.impression:
IMPRESSION:  Mild cardiomegaly with prominence of central pulmonary arteries,
likely indicates pulmonary arterial hypertension given the underlying
emphysema.  Please correlate clinically.  No evidence of pneumonia.

s56181233.txt.findings:
FINDINGS:  AP and lateral views of the chest are obtained.  Since the prior
study, there has been much improvement in the bilateral lung base
consolidations likely representing atelectasis.  There has also been
improvement in the previously seen bilateral pleural effusions.  There is no
evidence of congestive heart failure or prominent pulmonary edema.  There is
no new focal consolidation or evidence of pneumothorax.  The previously seen
left subclavian line remains in standard position, terminating in the upper
SVC.

CONCLUSION:  Much improved bilateral lung base atelectasis and bilateral
pleural effusions.  No new focal consolidation, pulmonary edema or
pneumothorax.
